title: Quantum Crossovers Revealed by Local Measurements
authors: A. C. S. Costa, E. C. Diniz, O. P. de Sa Neto
abstract: Quantum crossover phenomena play a central role in few-body open quantum systems, yet their identification often relies on global or model-dependent indicators. In this work, we demonstrate that crossovers can be robustly characterized through purely local measurements, establishing a direct connection between local quantum Fisher information and the onset of crossover behavior. We further demonstrate that quantum obesity does not, in general, generalize the quantum steering ellipsoid volume as a universal indicator of crossover. Instead, we identify regimes in which the ellipsoid volume remains insensitive to the transition, while the relevant signatures are encoded in the behavior of the local Bloch vector. These results reveal a geometric distinction between local and global indicators of crossovers.
summary: This paper develops a method to identify quantum crossover points in open quantum systems using only local measurements, rather than relying on global properties. By comparing local diagnostics like Quantum Fisher Information against global measures like the QSE volume, the authors prove that local structure is a more fundamental indicator of crossover. This offers a powerful, experimentally accessible tool for characterizing quantum phase transitions.
main_problem: The paper addresses the challenge of robustly characterizing quantum crossover phenomena in open quantum systems, moving beyond reliance on global or model-dependent indicators.
main_result: It establishes that quantum crossovers can be robustly characterized using purely local measurements, specifically linking the onset of crossover to the behavior of the local Quantum Fisher Information (QFI) and the local Bloch vector.
method: The authors employ theoretical analysis using a tripartite model, comparing local measurement diagnostics (QFI, local Bloch vectors) against global indicators (Quantum Steering Ellipsoid volume, Quantum Obesity).
model_or_system: The study uses a minimal tripartite model of three qubits (A, B, C) governed by a Hamiltonian and subjected to dissipative dynamics described by a Microscopic Master Equation (MME). The system is analyzed at zero temperature.
key_observables: Quantum Fisher Information (QFI), local Bloch vectors, Quantum Steering Ellipsoid (QSE) volume, and Quantum Obesity (QO).
important_parameters: Tunable couplings ($J, J_C$), system frequencies ($\omega_0, \omega_C$), and the control parameter driving the crossover.
main_assumptions: The analysis is performed on the global ground state $|\psi_Gangle$ and assumes the system dynamics are well-described by the MME.
figures_summary: Figures schematically illustrate the tripartite system and various observation schemes (full system, bipartitions). Other figures compare the behavior of QO and QSE volume against local measurement indicators as a coupling parameter is varied.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the physical model and the problem of crossover identification. It then develops and compares multiple diagnostic tools—local QFI vs. global QSE volume—to demonstrate the geometric distinction between these indicators.
why_interesting: This work is highly relevant as it provides a rigorous, local diagnostic tool (QFI) to pinpoint critical quantum behavior in open systems, which is crucial for designing quantum sensors and understanding decoherence effects.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Schematic representation of the tripartite system composed of three qubits A, B, and C. Qubits A and B are identical and interact via a symmetric signal coupling of strength J. Both qubits are independently coupled to the third qubit C through probe couplings of strength JC. The figure illustrates the different observation schemes considered in this work: (i) the full tripartite system, (ii) the bipartition AB obtained by tracing out qubit C, and (iii) the biparti- tions AC and BC, obtained by tracing out one of the iden- tical qubits. These configurations allow the analysis of local measurements, bipartite correlations, and symmetry-induced effects within a unified framework.

FIG. 3. Linear Entropy vs JC, with fixed parameters ωC = 5.0 and ω0 = 0.1 for all curves. The continuous curves represent EC|AB for parameters J = 0.1 (red curve), and J = 1 (blue curve). The non-continuous curves represent EB|AC for the parameters J = 0.1 (dotted curve), and J = 1 (dashed curve).

FIG. 2. (a) Quantum obesity and (b) Volume of the quantum steering ellipsoid as a function of JC. The continuous curves represent ΩAB for parameters J = 0.1 (red curve), and J = 1 (blue curve). The non-continuous curves represent ΩAC for the parameters J = 0.1 (dotted curve), and J = 1 (dashed curve).

FIG. 4. Local vector vs JC: The continuous curves repre- sent the magnitude of the local vectors ⃗yA(B) for parameters J = 0.1 (red curve), and J = 1.0 (blue curve). The non- continuous curves represent |⃗yC| for the parameters J = 0.1 (dotted curve), and J = 1 (dashed curve).

FIG. 5. Fisher information vs JC, with fixed parameters ωC = 5.0 and ω0 = 0.1 for all curves. The continuous curves represent FA(B) for parameters J = 0.1 (red curve), and J = 1 (blue curve). The non-continuous curves represent FC for the parameters J = 0.1 (dotted curve), and J = 1 (dashed curve).
